channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: Microsoft Is Testing Claude Against Its Own Copilot. Here's Why.
executive_summary: Microsoft is testing Claude against its own Copilot to address the gap between AI tool performance and actual work requirements. Many employees feel the corporate default tools are inadequate, leading to inefficiencies and frustration. The video outlines how to measure performance differences and effectively advocate for better tools within an organization.
bullets: The corporate default AI tool is often inadequate for specific tasks, leading to inefficiencies. Employees recognize that using a tool that doesn't fit their needs is like trying to cut a steak with a butter knife — it just won't work well. This mismatch can result in wasted hours and frustration.

Many employees are using alternative AI tools like Claude or ChatGPT on personal accounts because the corporate default is insufficient. This is akin to using a personal GPS app because the company-provided map is outdated and inaccurate. Such workarounds indicate a deeper issue with tool selection.

The statement 'Copilot is bad' fails to communicate the real problem effectively. It's like saying a car is slow without explaining that it can't even drive up a hill. Instead, employees should quantify how much time the default tool costs them compared to a specialist tool.

Measuring the time spent on tasks with different AI tools can reveal significant performance gaps. If the default tool takes four extra hours a week to complete a task, that’s like paying for a luxury car but only getting a bicycle. This data can help make a compelling case for better tools.

When asking for a new tool, it's crucial to reframe the request to avoid sounding like a complaint. Instead of saying 'this tool is bad,' frame it as 'for this specific job, the default tool costs us four extra hours a week compared to a specialist.' This approach is more likely to resonate with decision-makers.

To run an effective test, select a job that meets four criteria: it should run weekly, take at least 30 minutes, you should know what good output looks like, and it should have a real audience. Think of it like choosing a recipe that you know well and can easily judge the outcome — it makes for a fair comparison.

The evidence gathered from testing different tools should be extrapolated across the organization. If one team member saves four hours a week using a specialist tool, that could translate to significant savings across the entire department. It's like finding a leak in a dam — fixing it can save a lot of water over time.

The video highlights a real-world example where Claude produced a prototype in about an hour, which took a team a year to build. This is like a chef creating a gourmet dish in minutes while others take days to prepare a similar meal. Such examples can illustrate the potential of specialist tools.

The argument for better tools must be framed in terms of measurable outcomes rather than personal preferences. If the output from the specialist tool is usable right away, it shifts the conversation from taste to performance. Companies can ignore preferences, but they must acknowledge performance gaps.

When discussing new tools with different levels of management, tailor your message accordingly. At the IC level, present specific data; at the director level, propose a pilot; and at the executive level, suggest commissioning broader measurements. It's like adjusting your pitch based on your audience — what works for one group may not work for another.

Expect objections such as 'we already paid for it' or 'this is shadow IT.' Respond by emphasizing that the incremental cost of a specialist tool can be justified by the time saved. It's like arguing that spending a little more on a high-quality tool can save you money in the long run.

The video emphasizes that talent is moving to companies with better AI tools. If your organization doesn't adapt, it risks losing valuable employees to competitors who provide the tools they need. This is like a sports team losing its best players because they don't have the right equipment to win.
key_takeaways: Measure the time spent on a specific job using both the default and specialist AI tools. This data will help you quantify the performance gap and make a stronger case for better tools. Without this evidence, your argument may be dismissed as mere preference.

Reframe your request for a new tool by focusing on specific job performance rather than criticizing the default tool. This approach is more likely to gain traction with management and can lead to a more productive conversation. If you simply complain, you risk being ignored.

Select a job that runs weekly and is visible to others for your test. This will ensure that the results are meaningful and can be presented as evidence to support your request for a new tool. If the job is not significant enough, your findings may not be taken seriously.

technical_terms.Copilot: Microsoft's AI tool designed to assist users in various tasks. Think of it like a co-pilot in an airplane, helping the main pilot navigate but not always having the best skills for every situation.
technical_terms.Claude: A specialist AI tool that has shown superior performance in certain tasks compared to corporate defaults. Think of it like a Swiss Army knife, equipped with the right tools for specific jobs, unlike a basic pocket knife.
technical_terms.AI transformation: The process of integrating AI tools into business operations to enhance productivity and efficiency. Like upgrading from a flip phone to a smartphone, it changes how you interact with technology.
technical_terms.shadow IT: Using unauthorized software or tools without the company's approval. Think of it like sneaking snacks into a movie theater — it may be convenient, but it’s against the rules.
technical_terms.procurement process: The method by which companies acquire goods and services, including software tools. Like a formal shopping list, it ensures that purchases are planned and approved.
technical_terms.performance gap: The difference in effectiveness between two tools or methods. Think of it like comparing a high-speed train to a bicycle — one gets you to your destination much faster.
technical_terms.measurement: The act of quantifying performance to evaluate effectiveness. Like weighing ingredients for a recipe, precise measurement leads to better results.
technical_terms.extrapolate: To extend findings from a small sample to a larger population. Think of it like tasting a spoonful of soup to predict how the whole pot will taste.
technical_terms.success criteria: The standards used to evaluate the effectiveness of a task or tool. Like setting a finish line in a race, it defines what success looks like.
technical_terms.data residency: The physical or geographical location where data is stored and managed. Think of it like keeping your valuables in a safe — where it is located matters for security.
technical_terms.integration work: The effort required to connect different systems or tools to work together. Like assembling a puzzle, it takes time and effort to fit all the pieces together.
technical_terms.quality score: A rating that reflects the effectiveness of a tool's output. Think of it like grading a paper — it shows how well the work meets expectations.
technical_terms.incremental cost: The additional expense incurred for a specific action or decision. Like the extra cost of adding a topping to a pizza, it’s a small amount that can have a big impact.
technical_terms.job class: A category of work that shares similar characteristics or requirements. Think of it like grouping all your favorite books by genre — it helps you find what you need faster.
technical_terms.specialist tool: An AI tool designed for specific tasks, often outperforming general-purpose tools. Like a scalpel in surgery, it’s precise and effective for particular jobs.
technical_terms.pilot: A trial run of a new tool or process to evaluate its effectiveness. Think of it like a test drive for a car — it helps you decide if it’s the right fit before committing.
technical_terms.retention problem: The risk of losing valuable employees due to dissatisfaction with tools or processes. Like a leaky bucket, if you don’t fix it, you’ll keep losing water (or talent).
technical_terms.vendor consolidation: The practice of reducing the number of suppliers a company works with for efficiency. Think of it like cleaning out your closet — fewer items make it easier to find what you need.
